chunk_summary: This section discusses equity in Indian tele-nutrition, emphasizing the digital divide, caregiver capability, and language barriers as clinical variables affecting adherence, follow-up, and safety. It highlights tailored approaches for low-tech settings and resource-constrained families.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of the digital divide on tele-nutrition adherence and safety in India.
Identify strategies to adapt tele-nutrition services for low-tech and low-literacy families.
Learn how to document and address barriers like food insecurity and caregiver constraints.
Explore ethical considerations for privacy and confidentiality in tele-nutrition for adolescents.
key_facts: The digital divide directly affects adherence, follow-up completion, and safety in tele-nutrition.
Low-tech pathways include short audio calls, pictorial meal guidance, and local measurement points.
Families with food insecurity require 'lowest-cost acceptable diet' options and community support linkage.
Privacy concerns in crowded households necessitate scheduling sensitive discussions at safe times.
Clinical standards for triage and escalation should remain consistent across high-tech and low-tech pathways.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: WHO guideline: 'Digital interventions should be designed to ensure equitable access and address barriers such as literacy, connectivity, and privacy.' (Source: https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789241550505)
document_key_topics: Adapting care for low-tech and low-literacy settings
Addressing food insecurity and caregiver constraints
